exemplar.title: M25 #6: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #6: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

strengths: Directly addresses the prescribed title and selects two appropriate areas of knowledge (natural sciences and the arts).
Provides illustrative real-world examples (Pasteurâs germ theory, the discovery of DNA, Van Goghâs works) to ground claims.
Maintains a minimal coherent structure that links back to the central knowledge question.
weaknesses: Discussion remains largely descriptive, with assertions not developed through sustained analysis.
Arguments are often unclear or only loosely connected to the central question, relying on broad generalisations.
Examples are used illustratively rather than analytically, limiting their epistemic significance.
Minimal evaluation of different points of view and limited exploration of implications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The strand assessment places the essay in the 3â4 band: it is connected to the title and areas of knowledge but is mainly descriptive with limited, under-developed arguments and minimal evaluation of alternative perspectives. Allocating 3 marks reflects its position at the lower end of this band, acknowledges the strengths in structure and example use, and accounts for its significant weaknesses in depth and clarity. This distribution meets the compulsory total of 3 marks.

annotations.comment: The chronology of DNA discovery spans decades but lacks critical assessment of how epistemic barriers differed between 19th and 20th centuries; contrast these explicitly.
Citation format is inconsistent and URLs lack retrieval dates; ensure all references adhere to a single academic style and include access dates for online sources.
The discussion of âdestroyâ versus âlimitâ lacks conceptual precision. Define exactly what âdestroyâ entails epistemically within each AOK to avoid ambiguity.
The phrase âover-obtainmentâ is imprecise; consider using more standard terminology such as âexcessive accumulation of information.â

The phrase âscientists could not let their imagination flourishâ is an oversimplification; consider how models and hypothetical reasoning continue to inspire wonder even with established theories.
The thesis remains diffuse; refine your central claim into a concise argument and outline the main points you will explore for each area of knowledge.
The assertion that the arts and natural sciences âlargely differâ is stated without justification; provide an early roadmap that outlines these epistemic differences.
The final sentence is incomplete (âestablishing new.â); complete the thought and ensure all sentences are grammatically whole.
The term âintellectual exerciseâ is used without unpacking; define what distinguishes an intellectual from an emotional response in the context of art appreciation.
The conclusion summarizes key points but does not reflect on implications for future knowledge production; add a reflection on how wonder might guide ongoing inquiry.
The introduction effectively situates the discussion by defining key terms such as âacquiring knowledgeâ and âsense of wonder,â providing a clear foundation for subsequent analysis.
This paragraph ends abruptly mid-sentence; ensure that each claim is fully developed and transitions clearly to maintain coherence.
The miasma theory example is descriptive but lacks analysis of how Pasteurâs experimental methodology exemplifies knowledge construction in the natural sciences.
The claim that certainty âdestroys wonderâ overgeneralizes scientific practice; discuss the provisional nature of theories and how ongoing inquiry sustains wonder.
This paragraph is overly long and lacks clear signposting; break it into focused sub-claims to improve structure and readability.
Source Tulchinsky is cited parenthetically without evaluating its credibility; integrate a brief assessment of the sourceâs reliability.
Provide a more nuanced explanation of how initial mystery in science fosters multiple theoretical perspectives versus a single definitive explanation.
The counterargument that wonder motivated Pasteurâs discovery is noted but not developed; evaluate wonderâs dual role as both motivator and potential limitation in scientific advancement.
The DNA case study recounts chronology without analyzing its epistemological implications; explain how overcoming barriers rekindles wonder within the scientific community.
The link between technological advancement and renewed wonder is asserted but unsupported; include a modern example to illustrate how new tools reopen epistemic questions.
The transition from Pasteurâs discovery to the DNA example feels abrupt; add signposting to clarify the shift between these two case studies.
The essay effectively contrasts how interpretation in the arts can both reinforce and undermine wonder by linking emotional response to knowledge acquisition.
The paragraph reiterates earlier points about overanalysis without adding new insight; refine this section by introducing a novel nuance or counterargument.
The Potato Eaters example is illustrative but not critically examined; discuss how contextual knowledge alters the artworkâs significance as a knowledge claim.
Consider including counterexamples in the artsâsuch as literary criticismâthat show how analysis might equally limit wonder to strengthen your balanced evaluation.
The critique of overanalysis in Starry Night is valid but relies on a single source; engage multiple perspectives to evaluate how intellectual scrutiny might reduce emotional engagement.
The bibliography omits key details such as publisher information and retrieval dates for web sources; complete each entry for greater academic rigour.

The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
